text: Hi, David.
Hey, how are you doing?
How are you doing, sir?
I am doing pretty well.
Happy Wednesday.
I apologize.
No worries.
Understood.
Yeah, it's definitely a Wednesday in all senses of the word.
Just enough into the week to realize how far behind you are.
Hello, hello.
Happy to have a chance to chat.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
Previous to GrowthX, I did some of the work at startups.
And previous to that, I was the editor in chief of TechCrunch for about 10 years.
So a lot of editorial work.
at

There, obviously, running a news team, and then running programming for our global events like Disrupt, and then also Startup Battlefield, and then a bunch of other content work around dozens of other editorial projects and products that we launched over the years.

So many years in the content world, and then kind of came around, got introduced to Marcel and Daniel here at GrowthX, and really kind of liked the way that they were thinking about content and kind of atomizing it and breaking it down in ways that I had thought about it for many years.
But frankly, either the technology was not there or the time and money weren't there for me to execute on and build on.
So very, very glad to have the chance to do that now.
So that's my story.
It's how I ended up here.

And my job is to, you know, kind of shepherd the content and the quality of it, and then to understand how the tooling that we're building is articulating and kind of coming together in ways that support that.
So I helped it deliver content.
On time, but also at high quality and at scale, in order to do that, we just have to kind of create that feedback loop between, okay, it's off here.
Let's figure out how to improve that process, build that into the product, and then go for another round, right?
So that's what I'm doing here.
So pleased to meet you, and then happy to hear a little bit about you.
Yeah, sure.
So right now, for the last two years, I've been on my own, basically creating mostly thought leadership content for companies.
My clients include software companies, and a couple of the big consulting firms, and some smaller companies doing a variety of things.
Basically create articles for them, infographics, videos, et cetera, and also work on their content strategies.
You know, where should all these things play?
How do they play together?
You know, create...
Creating a cohesive narrative around all of their content.
I have contractors that I use for leverage, but no full-time employees.
And of course, increasingly, I'm using AI as a helper for myself, and also educating my clients on ways that they can use it to help their content creation efforts.
Before that, I was at McKinsey.
I was the executive editor for AI for six years, and that work included much of the similar things that I described, basically, but just doing it within McKinsey.
And prior to that, I was at IBM, somewhat similar, a little bit more on the content marketing side than content creation.
Creating the campaigns and such based off of content creation of others.
And you.
you.
you.
to that, I was at a market research firm for financial services.
Again, a lot of content marketing.
They had no content marketing program.
So I basically helped them build it up from scratch.
So I've been in the space for 15 years, seeing all the changes and a variety of subject matters that I've worked on.
But largely, AI and financial services would be my specialties.
Cool.
Yeah, that's great.
Yeah, we have a need for people, I think, that are in the same world as you, that have the content chops sort of in the hit pocket.

You need to understand what good content looks like and feels like to be able to judge, you know, kind of the out, not just obviously client deliverables, which is table stakes, but also the workflows themselves and the pipelines and how they're working to produce it.
I think the simplest, like, not ever...
Anybody has this kind of component to it is the ability to articulate why a piece of content is or is not working.
Like that's probably the most important content skill.
We would expect anybody here to be able to put together a decent copy or know what that looks like.
But it is really more in the realm of the articulation aspect of it that's super important to us.
And you've probably worked with many content writers over the years that are just brilliant and produce clean copy.
But if you ask them how they do it, they're like, I don't know, you know, like stuff goes in and then your good copy comes out.
those people aren't great, you know, but.
Yes.
Being an editor, you do have to learn how to articulate that.
And, you know, most of the time I've done that, you know, I would say on paper or on digital copy, you know, copy comes in, things aren't quite right.
I don't just clean it up.
I say, you know, here's why.
I'm doing this, or I think you should move this to the lead because here's why that's actually the right hook as opposed to what you have.
So that has definitely helped me think of my own processes, right?
Because previous to that, when I was just writing, I was kind of the same, right?
Saying, yeah, I do it and I'm not sure what I'm doing, but I'm doing it at the speed and quality that you need and everything's great.
But as soon as you transform into an editor, you know, that's not enough anymore.
Yeah, I think so.
And, know, it's helpful to like, to know what that feels like in that process.
So it's good that you do because right now, obviously some of that will still be directed at humans because there'll be humans using the workflows to produce content.
And you want to teach a person to fish, you know, for sure.
When you're building an editorial team and working with people, you generally want them to be better every day, not just a static state of, I guess.
As good as you're going to get.
So we'll just fix what your problems are, right?
But the same process goes towards the pipelines, right?
And the workflows themselves where the way we refer to pipelines and workflows, just to clarify, and I'm sure if you've worked with any workflow tools, you understand this.
But a workflow would be like a series of steps that get processed in order.
And then a pipeline is a series of workflows.
So building the workflows that take care of either content creation, editing, manipulation, things like linking or fact-checking or anything like that.
And then improving the quality of those enough to where we're happy with the outputs and understanding what the inputs are and articulating, okay, do we need new inputs?
Do we need to engineer the inputs better, right?
Going into this workflow.
So, and then the pipelines would be links of workflows together.
So a workflow could incorporate dozens of steps, but then a pipeline links dozens of workflows or.
A dozen workflows-ish on average together to create a desired outcome.

So perhaps a keyword or topic goes in and then a piece of edited, fact-checked, internally linked, maybe even externally linked output comes out the other end with even a generated set of images or image for either lead images or maybe even internal images.
And then any other particular requirements for that pipeline, like publishing or staging that happens in an automated fashion once it's released by a human reviewer.
Just to articulate, that's how we talk about pipelines and workflows.
This is very similar to the way everybody does.
However, I just throw that out there so that I'm not just throwing words into the ether.
But yeah, and so because we work in that world so much, I'd love to hear a little bit about like how you have used or have you used, if you have used, you know, kind of work.
Workflows yourself or pipelines of workflows even.

I think workflows more common and then people that have built pipelines of workflows are sort of the outer edge of that because it's just a little bit more of a nesting doll of, you know, actions you want to occur.
And so just curious about if you could articulate any of that that you've worked with and talk a little bit about that.
Yeah, well, when I was at McKinsey, we used Airtable.

So that was obviously a tool that we used that would basically bring you through our workflow, our editorial workflow, you know, starting with putting together the specs for the project and then the key deadlines and deliverables and then tracking that across editorial development.
And then, you know, kind of approvals, copy editing, proofreading, production, et cetera.
So that would all be set up through that tool.
Have I strung...
Them together?
No, I would say I've not strung it together.
mean, obviously, there are plugins and all those kinds of things that help out, but I've not actually like strung workflows together in the way that you're describing it.
You know, obviously, I'm using AI tools right now a good bit, kind of bumping in and out of them, right?
Like based on what I'm doing.
So, you know, if I'm developing copy, I'm usually using Claude, sometimes ChatGPT, fact checking.
I'll go back to ChatGPT if I was using Claude.
And, you know, I've got some agents that I've set up, one that kind of, I call it right like me, where I continually feed it my copy.
And so that every time I want to go in there and say, hey, take a look at this, you know, can you suggest areas for improvement or I'm struggling with the structure, you know, give me some ideas.
I've got an agent that'll kind of write it.
In my style already.
Yeah, good.
That makes sense.
Yeah, I think everybody should at the very least have a custom GPT trained on their writing because it's a good editing companion.
You know, I'll feed it pieces that I've written and I'm like, hey, well, you know, does this sound like me?
What do you think of this?
You know, and it's helpful.
It can be eye-opening.
Sometimes it's, sometimes it's, there's almost an antibody response.
If you train a GPT or a Claude instance well to write like you, sometimes you can be like, really?
That's how I sound?
Yeah.
Really?
You know, you don't want to believe it.
And then you look at it and you're like, I guess I, I guess I do say that.
So some, it can be helpful there sometimes.
And I use helpful like quotes because sometimes you don't want to hear it.
But, and then, okay, talk to me a little bit about like process, like times you work with.
And it's.
Especially writers or teams of writers or content creators.
Talk to me a little bit about your process, how you work with them to create content or, you know, keep the spice flowing for clients, that sort of thing.
Yeah, I mean, you know, it starts with the brief, right?
So I'm pretty religious about creating a brief up front, even for those that I've worked with for a long time.
Although maybe those will be a little more skeletal than ones that I might send to someone I haven't worked with before.
Or, and there's a lot of talking up front, you know, about positioning and client expectations and all of that.
And then it's a matter of iteration, right?
It's drafts coming back to me, taking a read through, giving direction, again, not trying not to touch the piece too much, especially initially, but more give guidance, you know, through feedback.
And then, you know, essentially coming to the...
You know, I've worked with so many writers over the time that I've been doing this.
Everyone's got a little bit of a different style.
So, you know, I try to adapt to what their style might be of learning and taking feedback.
You know, some are fine with you being ultra direct and knowing that you're speeding through things and some have to finesse it a little bit more to not ruffle their feathers.
Um, but I mean, that's generally been that.
And then, you know, if I, if times that I've had like a full team and we're doing different things like ideation sessions, obviously they're a bit of a different bear.
Um, and those would kind of be, you know, discussion whiteboarding type exercises and asking a lot of questions, you know, and doing a lot of listening to try to come to what we're trying to create.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Yeah.
mean, think it's, um, this role is like, it
All of that, you know, there is a client surface area.
The directors are generally kind of like the primary client-facing surface, but managing editors certainly are, you know, involved in client comms and talking to them.

And I think there's opportunities there to, you know, provide like a really significant opportunity to win and to like actually succeed in the scoping work and in clear client comms and expectation management, all that.
There's plenty of things we can do and we're good at it.
There are many things that we can do and we're bad at it, like bad in terms of like we're not the best of the world at it.
And if we're not, then we need to build the tooling to then be the best at it.
But managing client expectations around that kind of thing, it's super important.
So like work scoping is really important and then helping people to understand like.

What their expectations, what the deliverables are, and making sure that the translation work happens in both directions so that the content managers know what the expectations are and deliverables and clarity and deadlines.
But then on the client side, okay, when do we push back?

When do we tell them this is probably not going to play out the way that they think it is and maybe we should try it this way or just to honestly prevent scope creep because, you know, if somebody's paying you, as you probably will know in consulting, they're like, they're going to ask for more, always, right?
More and more and more.
And it's like, all right, when is this an opportunity for an upsell or renegotiating of the statement of work?
Or when is it an opportunity to like actually just be nimble in the normal way where we're like, hey, this is totally cool.
We can do this.
We're going to take existing effort that we're putting towards this and we're going to move it to this column.
Does that sound good?
Okay, cool.
Just so everybody's clear, less effort here, more effort here.
Let's go.
right, that's fine.
Or is it more like, this sounds awesome, but it also sounds like a lot of work.
And so we need to, because we don't want to deliver you any product that's half-baked.
We want to make sure that you're really happy.
And so I think that's part of the job as well, right?
Managing expectations internally and externally.
Got it.
Got it.
So what does a typical kind of day in the life of this role look like?
Yeah.
So, I mean, your week kicks off.
You probably want to, you know, review your scope of deliverables that are due that week to see, you know, just to plan out your editorial calendar.
Obviously, that would be planned out a little, hopefully longer than a week.
But I normally would come in, make sure that I'm reviewing all my deliverables for the week so that we know what our pacing needs to feel like.
Then understanding where those deliverables are coming from.
Do they involve design?
Do they involve product?
Or is it something that we're handling because it's already a steady state?
you?
Do
Deliverable, like something that we have built a pipeline out, a series of workflows out to deliver or get close to delivery.
And then interacting with the content managers that are producing those things to say, okay, you know, where are we at on this body of work?
Oh, it's ready for review.
Cool.
Let me dive in on that.
Spend some focus time making sure that client delivery is to expectation.
I see some things.
Let's kick that back down and maybe run some additional post-processing workflows on it.
Or actually, let's burn this one down and start over or, you know, whatever the case may be.
Doing, generally speaking, one, on average of one meeting per week per client that's public-facing, usually a 30-minute catch-up.
Unless it's a client that we're doing some big sprint project with or, you know, building a new product for.
Or we just want to get touch base with them, have a little FaceTime, making sure that they're seeing the deliverables that we are bringing to them for that week.
touch with them.
Or just Thank
And that they are happy with the quality and if there's any feedback or anything we need to take there, doing that sort of management and then kind of projecting them out for them.
Okay, cool.
So next week, next couple of weeks, here's what we're going to be working on.
Any asks of them.
Oh, we need assets or we need, you know, fonts or, you know, we'd love you to review X, or Z really closely because if you like it, we're about to make 10 more of them.
You know, things like that, that have added an effort, additive effort.
And then if we have those things in there in good relationship, we're usually in and out.
We communicate async with the clients the vast majority of time in a shared Slack channel and Notion space.
Then internally, your attention might turn towards one of your content managers having something that's broken or not working to spec in their pipeline.
And you can go like, cool.
Oh, I see where you're coming from.
And you're like.
Hey, have you checked these inputs and outputs?
Because I think there's something going on there.
they're like, ah, you know, okay, yeah, the inputs, we probably can work on that.
Whether that's like an artifact or the output of the previous step in the workflow.
And you're like, well, I can see where the, let's track where the issue actually is.
You know, is it in the drafting stage or maybe is it in the outline stage?
Or maybe is it even earlier in the assignment brief, right?
Where is it going astray?
Where is it not giving you the breadcrumb?
You know, tracking those breadcrumbs is important.
And the content managers, not all of them have worked with teams or built processes before.
So we don't expect them to be, some of them are great.
And some of them, you know, are really just big that way or have done this.
But we don't really expect that a lot of that to come out of that layer.
Instead, the Emmy layer is looking at it going, why are you having such a hard time?
Like, this seems like it should be doable within this timeframe.
Why are you struggling?
What's going on?
What can we get out of your way?
Or what's broken that we need to fix?
So our...
Articulating that, getting that, getting a ticket filed, prodding them for the outputs that you need from them to understand their issue and solving that and getting those blockers cleared.
And then a chunk of time probably utilizing the workflows and managing and massaging them yourself, either in a case where we're building something new for a client.
So we've got like a newer workflow that you want to really do a close read on.
Like I always, anytime we're using a new pipeline, a new workflow, if it's a client I'm helping out with, I close read the first five or six pieces of content from it.
Like with a, you know, I zap root of them.
I go over them with microscope just because I really want to understand where the broken bits are and really go close because I know what will happen.
You know, the natural human thing, once you get in the flow, everybody's just producing stuff.
And now you have a hundred copies where the letter E is squashed.
You know, like this is the old typesetter rule.
Like check your typeset, you know.
And like, that's the way I look at it.
And then, you know, doing a little bit of that.
And then some internal meetings on things like expressing the same kind of thing internally where you're like, hey, you know what we're having an issue with?
Is anybody else seeing this?
Do a little mind share with other MEs or other people on the delivery team.
And that's kind of like rounds out a week, right?
Like that's what a good chunk of it is.
There is, of course, always like the standard content stuff, which is like, oh, we have a new cluster of content that the client wants us to work on.
Let me run like just a basic keyword analysis in SEMrush to kind of get an understanding of is this tackling, is this targeting the kind of keywords that they want?
And now we've got a new cluster that we can drop into Airtable and into the client space for their review.
And then we'll tag them up in the internal channel and say, hey, we've got a new cluster.
I know you wanted to work on, you know, engineering, engineers and architects for this next cluster of content.
And we have created a keyword cluster.
Love to get you to glance at that.
And they're like, yeah, this all sounds.
Good.
Great.
We'll come back to you with topics next week.
And then, you know, you'll have a list of topics to review.
And then, of course, it advances from there to then generating articles or outlines for them to review.
And then hopefully moving on to, you know, final edits and publishing and that sort of thing.
So there'll always be a little bit of that going on on a per client basis.
I think that covers like the majority of like the daily stuff.

And then, you know, the outside of the box stuff is that they largely fall into the delta between what are the current, what is the current product, our product, growthx's product, delivering, and what is the delta between there and something that we would be more than happy to present to a client.
Like, what is the differential between these two states?
And there will always be some.
And hopefully it falls within that 95th percentile where a human review can take it there.
And we're like, cool, I spent about 10 to 15 minutes going over.
With this, adding some taste touches, things that we like, we know the client loves, and maybe our automated systems missed, or just doing a little bit of a taste pass yourself.
And you're like, you know what, I'm going to tweak this.
I think this is better, right?
Whatever that delta needs to be.
If we're within that percentage, we're good.
Like, that is a steady state client.
If, however, that delta is bigger, then maybe we need to figure out a way.
If it's anything you do more than once or twice a week, we really invest a lot of agency into, especially our emi layer and director layer, into figuring out what kind of process might work there.
Utilize whatever tooling we have.
We have subscriptions to basically all of the major AI tools and workflow tools.
Or if it's something you've used personally, cool.
We're agnostic.
We don't care, you know.
If it's a process you can create to fix that problem, obviously it's a win in the near term, because we continue to deliver high quality content, you know, high quality.
It's client work to spec, and the trains keep rolling, but then longer term, it's, hey, we built this process.
Maybe it's like a pipeline tool like an N8N or make.com or Zapier or something, and it calls an LLM at this step and then drops it into a Google Doc at this step or whatever.
Or even if it's something as simple as a cloud project with a certain set of prompts that you're like, hey, all I had to do was run these and it got us where we needed to get.
But then we're able to articulate that to product in the form of a ticket, usually a linear ticket.
It's a file that says, hey, we have a delta here.
It's bigger than it needs to be.
We're fixing this kind of one thing over and over and over and over.
Here's a process that we prototyped, and it got us to where we need to get.
We're happy with what's coming out of this.
Can we build this into a workflow, which we can then place in our pipeline, and now we don't have to do that anymore.
And therefore, the delta goes from here to there, and then so on and so forth.
Until you hit that 95th.
That's the process.

So you would be involved in that as well, either articulating or helping to spec out or just rough out a quick process that creates basically a prototype that the engineers can use as a product spec to say, oh, yeah, I see what you mean here.
You want, this came in, you had these inputs, you had this articulation, aka prompting or workflows or whatever, and then this output.
And so they can then take that and then productize it, right?
Make it a workflow.
Got it.
Got it.
I love the kind of dual aspect of this role.
Even just the title, when I saw this role, I was like, okay, this is interesting because A, this is where the ball is moving and B, this is how my brain works and what I like to do.

As throughout my career, I've always wanted to do things that were tended to end up being technically beyond what was capable and would have no problem diving right in with the engineers and the web development teams, et cetera.
And figuring out, okay, like how do we.
We actually do this rather than, you know, change what we're doing because it doesn't match, you know, our templates or our CMS or whatever.
Like, how do we actually make this happen?
So not to mention, I love playing with all the tools and have familiarity with everything you just mentioned.
So, yeah, I love this kind of dual mandate.
Yeah, that's good.
I mean, look, the future of all work is more along the lines of how can we create more leverage, right?
Like, hey, I'm a person with a finite amount of mental and physical energy.
How can I create leverage for myself to make my existing workload feel manageable?
But then also beyond that, you realize, oh, wow, if I can, like, trivialize a previously complex process, now we have opportunity to do more.
And usually in the content world, that is scale.
You know, it's like, how can we produce either more pieces of content?
Or more complex pieces of content in the same time it would have taken us to do simpler pieces of content in the past.

Something that, say, integrates several complex data stores into one piece of cohesive content, like a programmatic SEO engagement that might take a client database, an API, editorial content, a page template, and a GitHub publishing workflow.
And mash those together into something where we can, over the space of a few weeks, create hundreds of pages of link magnet content for a client.
We had one example, we created a directory of voice agents for a client called VAPI.
So VAPI exists as basically a voice layer in between the...
Models, like Eleven Labs or one of the other fundamental or foundational voice layers.
And then the client or the public-facing interfaces, wherever those may be.
Are they a chat box?
Are they a voice call, a VoIP call?
Are they a cell phone call to a robot database, basically?
And their problem is that we're like, you can use this for anything.
And that's a hard problem to solve in the content world because you're like, oh, cool.
Let me just target everything and everyone, right?
That's easy, no problem, right?
The ultimate top of funnel is just a picture of the planet Earth, right?
And it's like, okay, but how do we create more surface areas and surface areas that are more likely to convert because they're hyper-specific?
Well, that requires scale.
Like there's no way you can like create five bucket pieces or bucket, you know, buckets of content that would work.
You need to create dozens or hundreds of buckets of.
That would capture people right at the point where they're like, this is for me.
This feels like it's for me.

It's like, why are you saying, let's hope that we can catch the right audience with these dozen pieces of content and spend hours on each when you can go like, hey, let's spend an enormous amount of time, frankly, and energy up front and then generate thousands of endpoints, hundreds of thousands of endpoints.
And that's kind of like the approach that we're taking.
So we took Vappy's API, we hooked it up to a front end that provided a kind of like a template button that allowed you to like start chatting with that voice agent.
And then we created content around each voice agent.
So we created the prompt and basic personality for each of the voice agents, use their API to actually enact that, to spin that up, and then integrated that with...
A templated page that had content around each of those types of voice agents.
Let's call it an answering service for gyms, for mothers with koalas as adopted children, right?
Whatever it needs.
It wasn't quite that narrow, but you get the idea.
Voice agents for healthcare services, for dental offices, for gyms, for automotive service clients.
Any of the ones that were important to them to start sweeping into a funnel, right?
Like, hey, we don't really have any exposure to these.
These people don't even know we exist, and we know we can help them, and we know that they would convert and that they would find the service useful.
So it's like, cool.
How do we target them?
By creating a page that feels like it was made for them.
Like, they land on it, and they're like, oh, look, it's not just a voice answering service for some other profession that I maybe...
We were able to adapt to me.
No, it's for my shop, you know, right here.
And how do I do it?
Oh, it's like four steps.
And here it is outlined specifically for automotive shops, not just general steps.
And then not only that, but I can have a live demo of talking to an automotive shop answering service right here, you know.
And like that kind of project is something that you can only do when you have the ability to already have like have that workflow mindset and the pipeline mindset.
So you're already building those things.
This is not like, cool, we'll throw 100 bodies at this over the course of six months and you're going to spend $10 million, you know.
It's like, no, like engage us, 20, 30, 40K a month.
We have the specialists who can build these things because we're already building a lot of this kind of stuff.
And so we can adapt existing pipelines and workflows over a sprint process, usually about 8 to 12 weeks.
And then when you come out of sprint, we'll keep chugging away and making that content for you.
And then once in a while, we'll check in and see what new.
So that's kind of the way we approach it.
Yeah, it's very cool.
Yeah, it's fun.

I know we're getting a little over, but I'm curious if you were to like, I don't know, if you had a pie of 100 and you were to kind of think about the types of content, or I guess it's content and tools that you're creating and that I would be kind of involved with.
Like, what would they fall into?
Like, something that you just mentioned versus like a blog?
Yeah.

Yeah, there are different kinds of like, you would probably be involved in some ways in work streams that are really about building like analytics, content, tooling, or building a content library as an example.

Like, don't want to use Airtable, not because Airtable is evil, it's just because we should probably own that, you know, for our clients, because there's so many things we want to do that Airtable is like, okay, you could do it.
In this really kludgy way, it's like, oh, you know, we'll just build that, right?
So we're building our own content library.
So input on how that works, you know, manipulating those things, giving feedback, going like, oh, you know what would be great?
You know, like that's why we have people working on these things internally.
Otherwise, we would just throw it out there and to the public and, you know, hopefully figure out PMF on the fly.
Then, you know, and things like geo, right?
Like there's no reason we can't do geo tracking.
That's actually relatively table stakes these days for search engines, search engine visibility.
There's, I would say that's maybe like, maybe even a 10% time is like helping to develop those products, understand them, manipulate them, give feedback on them, file tickets.
Then a good chunk of the time is in that Delta world that I mentioned.
Let's call it 25% of the time is like, why is this workflow broken?
What's going on?
Let's make sure it works right.
Right.
Or it's actually.
But I think it could work so much better if only XYZ, you know, that's a good chunk of time.
